citation: Nelson, A. (2023). Review of the book Let's roll: A guide to setting up tabletop role-playing games in your school or public library, by L. Maxwell. School Library Journal, 69(5), 103.
summary:
Nelson's short professional review evaluates Lucas Maxwell's *Let's Roll: A Guide to Setting up Tabletop Role-Playing Games in Your School or Public Library* for the May 2023 professional-reading section of *School Library Journal*. The review is not a research article, program case study, or direct account of a library TTRPG program. Its value for the GBLS project is instead bibliographic and field-facing: it records how a mainstream school-library review venue presented Maxwell's handbook to practitioners at the moment of publication.

The review frames Maxwell's book as a support text for library staff who are new to tabletop role-playing games and for staff who already understand gaming but feel uncertain about launching RPG programming. The named barriers are practical rather than theoretical: finding institutional support, marketing to patrons, and managing a chaotic program session. Nelson also identifies the book's supplemental materials, including a glossary of terms, a sample program proposal, case studies from school and public librarians, recommended reading lists, and brief reviews of simple gateway RPGs that require minimal preparation or expertise.

Nelson's assessment emphasizes tone and usability. The book is described as well organized, specific, gentle, and conversational. The review notes that Maxwell sometimes leans heavily on his own experience as a high school librarian, but it also highlights case study statements from librarians running TTRPGs in different library settings and with different age groups. The verdict recommends the title for school or public library professionals who feel trepidation about introducing an RPG program.

For the larger GBLS project, this review reinforces the practitioner-training problem around TTRPG services. It suggests that many librarians do not primarily need persuasion that TTRPGs exist; they need practical help translating the format into library workflows, administrative language, promotion, facilitation, and beginner-friendly game selection. The review also shows how professional literature positions TTRPG programming as relevant to both school and public libraries, and how anxiety about facilitation, institutional approval, and session management remains central to adoption.

At the same time, this item should not be used as substantive evidence in the literature review when Maxwell's handbook itself is available for direct processing. Nelson summarizes and recommends the book but does not add original data, independent program details, or a distinct theoretical argument. It is best retained as an adjacent professional-review record and a pointer to Maxwell (2023), not as a separate paragraph in `review.md`.

terosa. Deepening Digital Citizenship: A Guide
to Systemwide Policy and Practice. 168p. ISTE. Mar. 2023. pap. $39.99. ISBN 9781564849663. Digital citizenship, a nebulous and slipSHU\  WRSLF   LV  RIWHQ  GLIâFXOW  IRU  WHDFKHUV  DQG

librarians to implement. This text expounds on how to introduce digital citizenship from WKH WRS GRZQ DQG HQFRXUDJHV GLVWULFWV WR âQG  RXW  ZKDW  WKH\  GHâQH  DV  GLJLWDO  VFKRODUVKLS   what their needs are, and how to address the needs for the population they serve. For instance, if they have a large refugee population, or a strong neurodivergent contingent, districts can assess their needs and put goals in place for educators and leaders, and for what the students need at the practical level. The OHQV âUVW IRFXVHV RQ KRZ HGXFDWLRQDO OHDGHUV  can make systemwide change; then, on how to educate those who will implement the curriculum and what that looks like at the ground level. The authors encourage educators to analyze what their district or school needs and to work with every student in mind. Though the book is academic, it lacks the oppressiveness of academic writing. Packed with resources, standards, questions to help educators tailor their programs, and examples including case studies, this book attempts to demystify the topic of digital citizenship and what that means to education leaders as they sit down and try to address the needs of a district, school, or students. VERDICT Perfect for librarians who wear many hats and need to analyze the implementation of digital citizenship from every angle, as well as for educators who have to make district-wide decisions that impact many students. o5DFKHO -RLQHU
VOELS, Sarah. Auditing Diversity in Library Col-
lections. 160p. Libraries Unlimited. Aug. 2022. pap. $60. ISBN 9781440878749. An insightful book packed with helpful and timely information to aid librarians and library professionals who may be envisaging a diversity audit of their library collection. Voels begins by providing an overview of the history of publishing in relation to equity, diversity, and inclusion; the history of diversity audits; and how audits have impacted her library’s collection. Other chapters discuss audits done by public and school libraries across the FRXQWU\   ãDZV  DQG  OLPLWDWLRQV  RI  DXGLWV   DQG  how libraries have used audits to stimulate equity in other aspects, such as programming, instruction, and community relations. Each chapter ends with references, and an index and suggested resources are provided in the back of the book. A summary of audit advice from the libraries included in the book is also quite useful. VERDICT This informative and engaging book is a great choice for both library students and established public and school librarians.o6XVDQ .HWFKDP

Supplemental materials include a glossary of common terms, a sample program proposal, case studies from school and public librarians, several recommended reading lists, and brief reviews of a number of simple gateway RPGs that can be learned and played by librarians and patrons with minimal preparation or expertise.

Library_Context.school_library.quote: Packed with resources, standards, questions to help educators tailor their programs, and examples including case studies, this book attempts to demystify the topic of digital citizenship and what that means to education leaders as they sit down and try to address the needs of a district, school, or students.
